content.full_text:
APR 27 '93 03:34PM AT&T 9015PF P.1/6

City Of Calimesa

F. Thos. Caporael, City Attorney
1025 Calimesa Boulevard, Suite 3A
Calimesa, California 92320
Telephone (909) 795 1745
Fax (909) 795 2108

April 27, 1993

Mr. John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Fax: (916) 327 2026

Re: Your File Nos. I-91-374 and I-91-452

Dear Mr. Wallace:

As the City of Calimesa moves closer to adopting a mobilehome
rent stabilization ordinance, there is a need for a current opinion
from your office advising whether Mayor Shirley A. Morton is
disqualified to vote in favor or against such an ordinance.

The facts have not changed substantially since your letter to
then Councilmember Morton on October 17, 1991. (Your File No. I-
91-374.) However, on page 8 of your letter, you refer to the
population of Calimesa as being approximately 12,000, with
approximately 2018 senior citizens residing in mobilehome parks.
While these numbers may be correct, I cannot verify them. Calimesa
was incorporated on December 1, 1990, after the federal census of
the area. Consequently, we do not have an accurate census of the
City. For the purposes of state subventions, the state estimates
the population as three times the number of registered voters.
That estimates yields a population of 12,000. Nevertheless,
Caltrans has posted signs at the city limits stating the population
to be 8,000. Extension of the county planning department
demographics yields an estimated population of 10,000. Of course,
the higher the estimated population the smaller the percentage of
segment population significantly affected and the more conservative
your opinion, which is probably desirable.

I have approached the problem from a slightly different angle.
For the purposes of the City's general plan and mandatory trash
collection program, the city has inventoried the existing number of
dwelling units in the City and found there are 3050, including
mobilehomes. The mobilehome rent stabilization ordinance committee
conducted a survey of the eight mobilehome parks in the City and

P.O. Box 1190 • Calimesa, California 92320 • (714) 795-9801

[recycling symbol] Printed on Recycled Paper

APR 27 '93 03:35PM AT&T 9015PF P.276

John W. Wallace
April 27, 1993
Page 2

found there were 991 occupied mobilehomes in the City of which 483
were occupied pursuant to month to month tenancy and 508 were
occupied pursuant to long term leases. Spaces occupied pursuant to
long term leases are exempt from local control. (Civ. Code,
§ 798.17.) Therefore, only 483 mobilehome would be affected by a
mobilehome rent stabilization ordinance. This is 15.9% of rental
dwelling units in the City. This again is the most conservative
estimate of the percentage of households that would be affected by
a mobilehome rent stabilization ordinance. On this basis, I do not
believe Mayor is disqualified from voting on the issue.

Based on the information available to me, and your previous
letters, I have prepared a memorandum for the City Council which
includes my belief and is attached hereto for your perusal. I
would appreciate your opinion of my memorandum as it pertains to
Mayor Morton and any other advice or comments you care to make.

Thank you for your past courtesies and cooperation, and your
consideration of my current request.

Sincerely,

[signature]
F. T. Caporael
City Attorney

cc: Mayor Shirley A. Morton

408

71

Aug 4, 1991

Dear Mr. Wallace,

In reference to our telephone conversation of July 15,1991 on Conflict
of Interest.

I and my husband moved to Calimesa in 1985. We moved from living
in a home to a Mobile Home Park setting. At the time we moved into
the Park, we were given a lease of five or ten year to choose from.
We chose the ten year lease thinking we were protecting our
future. We were not given the option of a month to month lease.
We never knew it existed. I soon became aware of such things as
the Civil Code and Title 25 of the state of California and how they
protect your civil rights. I found that the Quality of Life is
not what you are living but what others give to you.

When Calimesa became a City, I decided to run for City Council. My
platform was to put an Ordinance in effect that would be fair and
equitable to both Residents and Park Owners. Park Residents need
to be protected and Park Owners need a fair return on their
investments.

At this time in Calimesa, we have eight mobile home parks with approx.
eight owners and approx. 2,013 senior residents living in these parks.
Because mobile homes are occupied by senior citizens, approx. eighty
years of age, persons of low or moderate income, living on fix
income, and because abuses are common regarding rules and
regulations along with rent increases, I felt it necessary to expedite
a Rent Stabilization Ordinance, with a Rent Board and an Administrator
who would mediate between the residents and park owners. The
Administrator would protect the Residents in all the Civil Codes.

I am a member of the G.S.M.O.L. and just because there is a State Law
to protect one's Civil Rights, dosen't necessarily mean it is protected
unless you, in your community, have another eloise vehicle by which
you can serve the people. Our eighty year old widows and widowers
intimidate easily.

Because I live in a Mobile Home Park and have for over six years now,
with four years left and knowing what the Quality of Life I live now,
a small group of mobile home residents and I have worked on the Rent
Ordinance. I did this as a private citizen, in my home and in others
home. I am not the head of this committee, we have a spokesperson.
As a Citizen, involved in Senior Affairs, I just helping elderly people,
give a much better Quality of Life. I am a Senior Activist.

I am now asking that as a City Council Person, that I may act on that capacity in dealing with this ordinance. You must remember, that as of now no matter what ordinance is put in place I will recieve no benefit from it. I have a long term lease and long term leases do not protect any person having this tool. Therefore, I benefit not, nor do I gain any advantages. I feel at this time I am not in Conflict of Interest with this ordinance.


This ordinance has been presented to the City Council just for consideration without any discussion thus far. This ordinance will be presented to the community at a type of town hall meeting the first week of September for discussion. I am now asking for your opinion as to may I participate in discussion at the Council Study Session, and participate in the voting when it becomes a part of the City Council Agenda. I realise I am asking for a request to bre issued to me in a short time, but time is of the essence for me.

1. A Rent Stabilization Ordinance was my platform when I was running for City Council before we became a City.

2. I helped work on this Ordinance as a private citizen and I didn't host this commitment.

3. I live in a Mobile Home Park for over six years (My home has been up for sale for a year now, I don't wish to live in one any longer)

4. I have a ten year lease, therefore disqualifying me from any protection under a lease unless laws in California change.

Please consider my request to participate in the discussion and voting on Calimesa's Rent Stabilization Ordinance.

If I have not given you enough information, please call me 714-795-4625, I will be happy to clear ANY matter to expidite my request.

Thank you,

[signature]

Shirley A. Morton
CouncilWoman


Shirley A. Morton
10961 Desert Lawn Dr. # W
Calimesa, Ca. 92220

sections.question_synthetic: The City Attorney requests FPPC advice on whether Mayor Shirley Morton is disqualified from voting on a proposed mobilehome rent stabilization ordinance, given that she resides in a mobilehome park but holds a long-term lease exempt from the ordinance's coverage.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains incoming request letters to FPPC, not FPPC response/advice letter. No formal Q/C sections present. References previous FPPC letter (File No. I-91-374 from October 17, 1991) but that letter is not included. Document dated April 27, 1993 but references 1991 telephone conversation and appears to include Mayor Morton's statement from an earlier date. Multiple fact scenarios presented but no FPPC conclusion provided in this document.; Date cleared: parsed 1993-04-27 but file year is 1991

embedding.summary: This document consists of two incoming requests to FPPC counsel regarding Mayor Shirley Morton's potential conflict of interest in voting on a mobilehome rent stabilization ordinance. The City Attorney requests clarification on disqualification based on updated population and housing unit data, while Morton herself argues she should be permitted to participate and vote, citing her long-term lease exemption and role as a senior activist.
